description:
You are a compliance analyst who must produce an auditable trail of user-login activity so that an external regulator can replay exactly what happened on 21 Apr 2023.  
Use only command-line tools that are normally present in a minimal Linux distribution (e.g. sqlite3, cat, echo, printf, etc.).

WHAT YOU MUST CREATE
1. Directory  
   • /home/user/compliance_audit/  
     (If it already exists, leave everything outside the scope of this task untouched.)

2. SQLite database  
   • /home/user/compliance_audit/audit.sqlite

3. Log file to hand to the regulator  
   • /home/user/compliance_audit/audit_20230421.log

DATABASE REQUIREMENTS
• The database must contain the following two tables and **only** these two tables.

  a) employee  
     Columns (and exact SQLite types):  
        id            INTEGER PRIMARY KEY  
        name          TEXT NOT NULL  
        department    TEXT NOT NULL  

  b) login  
     Columns (and exact SQLite types):  
        id            INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT  
        employee_id   INTEGER NOT NULL  
        login_ts      TEXT NOT NULL  
     Foreign-key relationship: login.employee_id → employee.id

• Populate employee with exactly three rows (id, name, department):
     1001 | Alice Smith   | Finance  
     1002 | Bob Jones     | HR  
     1003 | Charlie Young | IT  

• Populate login with exactly six rows (id is AUTOINCREMENT, so let SQLite assign them 1-6) using these timestamps (ISO 8601, UTC “Z” suffixed):

     employee_id | login_ts
     1001        | 2023-04-21T08:15:00Z
     1002        | 2023-04-21T08:45:00Z
     1001        | 2023-04-21T12:05:00Z
     1003        | 2023-04-21T13:25:00Z
     1002        | 2023-04-21T16:55:00Z
     1003        | 2023-04-21T18:07:00Z

AUDIT LOG FILE SPECIFICATION
After the database is created and populated, generate /home/user/compliance_audit/audit_20230421.log with the **exact** contents described here.  
The file is plain UTF-8 text.  
Lines 1-6 are comma-separated with no surrounding spaces and **no header line**. They must be ordered by login.id ascending (1 → 6).  
Field order per line:

    login_id,employee_id,employee_name,department,login_ts

After those six data lines:
• Insert one completely blank line (newline only).
• Then insert the literal header “# Summary” (without the quotes) on its own line.
• Then, on the following three lines, write department-level counts in the form:

    <Department>,<LoginCount>

These summary lines must be sorted alphabetically by department name.

When finished, the file should look exactly like:

1,1001,Alice Smith,Finance,2023-04-21T08:15:00Z
2,1002,Bob Jones,HR,2023-04-21T08:45:00Z
3,1001,Alice Smith,Finance,2023-04-21T12:05:00Z
4,1003,Charlie Young,IT,2023-04-21T13:25:00Z
5,1002,Bob Jones,HR,2023-04-21T16:55:00Z
6,1003,Charlie Young,IT,2023-04-21T18:07:00Z

# Summary
Finance,2
HR,2
IT,2

PERMISSIONS
• The new directory /home/user/compliance_audit must end up readable by the current user; default umask is acceptable.  
• No root access is needed; everything should be doable as the regular user.

HOW YOUR WORK WILL BE VERIFIED (for your information)
Automated tests will:
1. Check that /home/user/compliance_audit/audit.sqlite exists and is a valid SQLite database.  
2. Verify the exact schema of employee and login tables.  
3. Confirm that the rows exist with the exact values listed above.  
4. Check that /home/user/compliance_audit/audit_20230421.log exists and **matches byte-for-byte** the sample shown above (trailing newline at the very end is required).  

CONSTRAINTS
• Accomplish everything in no more than 10 reasonable shell commands (e.g. mkdir, sqlite3, echo/printf, etc.).  
• Do not manually edit binary files; rely on SQLite commands to build the database.  
• Avoid temporary files unless absolutely necessary.  

Once the directory, database and log file are in place and match the specification, you are done.
truth:
Expected artefacts after successful completion:

1. Directory:
/home/user/compliance_audit/   (exists)

2. Database file:
/home/user/compliance_audit/audit.sqlite
   a) Exact schema:
      CREATE TABLE employee(
         id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
         name TEXT NOT NULL,
         department TEXT NOT NULL
      );
      CREATE TABLE login(
         id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
         employee_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
         login_ts TEXT NOT NULL,
         FOREIGN KEY(employee_id) REFERENCES employee(id)
      );

   b) Rows:
   employee:
      (1001,'Alice Smith','Finance')
      (1002,'Bob Jones','HR')
      (1003,'Charlie Young','IT')
   login (explicit id values are 1-6 autogenerated in this order):
      (1,1001,'2023-04-21T08:15:00Z')
      (2,1002,'2023-04-21T08:45:00Z')
      (3,1001,'2023-04-21T12:05:00Z')
      (4,1003,'2023-04-21T13:25:00Z')
      (5,1002,'2023-04-21T16:55:00Z')
      (6,1003,'2023-04-21T18:07:00Z')

3. Log file:
/home/user/compliance_audit/audit_20230421.log
Exact ASCII/UTF-8 contents including final newline:

1,1001,Alice Smith,Finance,2023-04-21T08:15:00Z
2,1002,Bob Jones,HR,2023-04-21T08:45:00Z
3,1001,Alice Smith,Finance,2023-04-21T12:05:00Z
4,1003,Charlie Young,IT,2023-04-21T13:25:00Z
5,1002,Bob Jones,HR,2023-04-21T16:55:00Z
6,1003,Charlie Young,IT,2023-04-21T18:07:00Z

# Summary
Finance,2
HR,2
IT,2

(with a trailing newline after the last “IT,2” line)

The test harness will perform `sqlite3 /home/user/compliance_audit/audit.sqlite ".schema"` and compare it against the schema above (ignoring whitespace differences), query both tables to verify row contents, and then read `audit_20230421.log` to assert the exact byte sequence (including newlines) matches the block above.
